DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Aug-28-2018 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
APPEARANCES: Attys. JONATHAN M. COHEN, ESQ. and KRISTINA A. DEL VECCHIO, ESQ. of Joseph & Cohen (Tel: 415-817-9200) appearing for plaintiff San Francisco Federal Credit Union;
Attys. MATTHEW LEE, ESQ. and KENNETH M. WALCZAK, ESQ. of the City Attorney's Office of the City and County of San Francisco (Tel: 415-554-4670) appearing for defendant City and County of San Francisco.

The matter is argued and submitted. The Court adopts its tentative ruling. Defendant City and County of San Francisco's demurrer to all six causes of action in plaintiff San Francisco Federal Credit Union's first amended complaint is overruled as to the first, second and third causes of action for breach of contract, breach of the implied covenant and breach of a mandatory duty and sustained without leave to amend as to the fourth, fifth and sixth causes of action for breach of fiduciary duty, negligent misrepresentation and promissory estoppel. The Credit Union sufficiently alleges claims for breach of contract and breach of the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing. "While plaintiffs' interpretation of the contract may prove invalid, it [is] improper to resolve the issue against her solely on her own pleading. 'In ruling on a demurrer, the likelihood that the pleading will be able to prove his allegations is not the question.'" (Aragon-Haas v. Family Security Ins. Services, Inc., (1991) 231 Cal.App.3d 232, 239). The broad language of the contract is reasonably susceptible to the Credit Union's interpretation. As to the mandatory duty claim, taking the Credit Union's allegations as true, the Credit Union has sufficiently alleged that the City chose to terminate the transfer program without making payments contrary to its mandatory duty per SF Transportation Code 1116(d)(3). As to the fiduciary duty claim, section 10 of the parties' agreement states that the parties were not creating a joint venture or partnership and the conclusory allegations in the complaint do not show a fiduciary relationship. As to the negligent misrepresentation claim, the Credit Union's reliance on Arthur L. Sachs, Inc. v. Oceanside (1984) 151 Cal.App.3d 315, 320 is misplaced. That case held that immunity from liability for employee's misrepresentation per Government Code 818.8 was no bar to a contract action for rescission under Government Code 814. The Credit Union purports to allege a distinct tort claim for damages and is not seeking rescission. A plaintiff cannot state a claim for promissory estoppel when the promise was given in return for proper consideration. The claim instead must be pleaded as one for breach of the bargained-for contract. (Raedeke v. Gibraltar Savings & Loan Assn. (1974) 10 Cal.3d 665, 672-673; see Garcia v. World Savings, FSB (2010) 183 Cal.App.4th 1031, 1040-1041.) The Court signs a formal order.

Judge: Harold Kahn, Clerk: Sean Kane, Court Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR#8600, torreanom@aol.com, Reported. (302/HK)
